extra.message: User input from `form['user_id']` (POST parameter) is directly formatted into a raw SQL query string passed to `User.objects.raw()`. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL by providing a crafted `user_id` POST parameter, enabling data exfiltration or modification.

User-controlled `ip` and `col` parameters are directly interpolated into a raw SQL query using `%` string formatting, then executed via `connection.cursor().execute(cmd)`. The `ip` value comes from `request.GET['ip']` (app/views/admin/views.py:98) and `col` from GET parameter keys (app/views/admin/views.py:88-93). An attacker can inject SQL via the `ip` query parameter (e.g., `?ip=' OR '1'='1`) or via specially crafted GET parameter names.

User-controlled `classname` from `request.GET.get('class')` is passed directly to `eval()` on line 16, and again on line 27. This allows arbitrary Python code execution by providing a crafted `class` query parameter (e.g., `?class=__import__('os').system('id')`). No authentication is required.
User-controlled `classname` from `request.GET.get('class')` is passed directly to `eval()` on line 27. Same code injection vulnerability as line 16, in the `api_id` endpoint.

User-controlled filename from `uploaded_file.name` (originating from `request.FILES['myfile'].name` in app/views/users/benefit_forms/views.py:43) is interpolated into an `os.system()` call. An attacker can upload a file with a crafted filename containing shell metacharacters (e.g., `file.pdf; rm -rf /`) to execute arbitrary OS commands.

User-controlled `name` query parameter from `request.GET.get('name', '')` is directly concatenated into a file path and then passed to `file(path)` and `os.path.getsize(path)`. An attacker can use `../` sequences to read arbitrary files on the filesystem (e.g., `?name=../../etc/passwd`).

User-controlled `encoded_user` from `request.POST['user']` is base64-decoded and then deserialized via `pickle.loads()`. An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload and pass it as the `user` POST parameter to achieve arbitrary code execution. Additionally on line 67-68, a user-controlled token is also used in a pickle.dumps() call, which feeds this insecure deserialization chain.

The `safe_name()` method uses Django's `mark_safe()` on `self.first_name`, marking user-controlled first name content as safe HTML. If a user sets their first name to contain JavaScript (e.g., `<script>alert('XSS')</script>`), it will be rendered without HTML escaping in any template that calls `{{ user.safe_name }}`, leading to stored cross-site scripting.

The `path` parameter from `request.POST['path']` (line 27) is directly used as the redirect target in `HttpResponseRedirect(path)` (line 43) with no validation. An attacker can craft a login form submission with a malicious `path` value (e.g., `https://evil.com`), redirecting authenticated users to an external phishing site after login.

All admin views use only the `@user_is_authenticated` decorator, which only checks for a valid `auth_token` cookie. None of the admin views verify `user.is_admin`. Any authenticated non-admin user can access admin dashboard, view/edit/delete any user, and view analytics by navigating to `/admin/{id}/update_user/` or similar admin endpoints.

The `user_work_info_index` function uses `User.objects.get(id=user_id)` where `user_id` comes directly from the URL pattern without verifying that the authenticated user matches this ID. Any authenticated user can view any other user's work information (including SSN data) by changing the user_id in the URL.

The `user_messages` function takes `creator_id` and `receiver_id` from `request.POST` (lines 26-28) without verifying that `creator_id` matches the currently authenticated user. An authenticated user can create messages impersonating any other user by providing arbitrary `creator_id` and `receiver_id` values in the POST body.

A hardcoded AES encryption key `b'12345678910111212345678910111212'` is defined in settings. This key is used for encrypting sensitive data (SSN, bank account numbers) via the Encryption utility. A static key means all encrypted data can be decrypted if the source code is obtained, compromising all encrypted sensitive user data.

Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in settings.py. This key is used for cryptographic signing (sessions, CSRF tokens, etc.). An attacker with access to the source code can forge session cookies, CSRF tokens, and other signed data.

ACCESS_TOKEN_SALT is hardcoded as `S4828341189aefiasd#ASDF` in settings. This salt is used when validating API authorization tokens. A hardcoded salt weakens the token validation scheme and enables token forgery.

DEBUG is set to True in production settings. This causes Django to display detailed traceback information, database queries, settings, and environment variables on error pages, leaking sensitive information to users.

User passwords are hashed using MD5 (`hashlib.md5()`), which is cryptographically broken and vulnerable to collision and preimage attacks. An attacker who gains access to the password database can efficiently crack passwords using rainbow tables and GPU-based brute force.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: Authentication tokens are generated using MD5 with only ~1 million possible random values (`random.randint(1, 1000000)`). The combination of weak hashing and low entropy makes auth tokens predictable and brute-forceable, enabling session hijacking.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: Password reset tokens are generated using MD5. The token is constructed as `user_id + '-' + MD5(email)`. Since MD5 is weak and the email may be enumerable, an attacker can forge password reset tokens for arbitrary users.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: The `WorkInfo.__str__()` method includes the raw SSN (`self.SSN`) in the string representation. When a WorkInfo object is logged (e.g., via Django error logging, debug output, or exception messages), the SSN will be written to logs or displayed in error pages, resulting in sensitive data exposure.

The `Pay.__str__()` method includes raw bank account and routing numbers in the string representation. When a Pay object is logged, these financial details will be exposed in logs, debug output, or error messages.
